definition: How much cash the company spends per month (or week). At Acme, approved guidance is $850K–$1.0M/month (~$196K–$231K/week). Current actual burn is ~$1.34M/month — 34–58% above target.
implication: Burn above guidance shortens runway and signals spending discipline problems. A CFO would investigate which departments or one-time events (conferences, hiring) drove the overage.

definition: Months until cash runs out at the current burn rate. Formula: Total Cash ÷ Monthly Burn. Acme target: 18+ months. Current: ~9.5 months at ~$1.34M/mo burn on $12.68M cash.
implication: Below 12 months triggers board conversations about fundraising, cost cuts, or revenue acceleration. Runway is the single number investors ask first.

definition: Cash in the day-to-day operating account used for payroll, vendors, and expenses. At Acme: $11.18M of the $12.68M total.
implication: Operating cash is what you actually spend from. Reserve cash is intentionally locked away — don't confuse total cash with spendable cash.

definition: Cash set aside in a separate account, not for daily operations. At Acme: $1.5M reserve.
implication: Reserves provide a buffer for emergencies or covenant compliance. Drawing on reserve is a deliberate CFO decision, not routine spending.

definition: Cash spent in a single week. Acme tracks 8 weeks of history. Current: ~$310K/week vs guidance of ~$196K–$231K/week.
implication: Weekly view catches spikes faster than monthly — e.g. a $77K conference overage (MODEX + ProMat) shows up immediately in the burn bar chart.

definition: Money customers owe Acme for goods or services already delivered. Outstanding invoices until paid.
implication: AR is not cash — it's a promise. Overdue AR tightens cash flow because you've already spent to deliver but haven't been paid.

definition: Money Acme owes vendors for goods or services received. Open vendor invoices until paid.
implication: Paying AP reduces cash. Paying duplicate or fraudulent AP wastes cash. Holding disputed AP preserves cash but risks vendor relationships.

definition: Average days to collect payment after invoicing. Healthy B2B: 30–45 days. Above 60 days signals collections problems.
implication: High DSO means you're effectively lending money to customers. Every day of DSO above terms is working capital trapped.

definition: Grouping invoices by how overdue they are: Current, 1–30 days, 31–60, 61–90, 90+ days.
implication: The older the bucket, the lower the probability of full collection. 90+ day invoices often need escalation, payment plans, or write-offs.

definition: Difference between planned budget and actual spending. Positive variance (over budget) = spent more than approved.
implication: Persistent over-variance in one department means either the budget was wrong, spending is out of control, or both. CFOs reforecast when variance exceeds ~10%.

definition: Comparison of what a department was approved to spend (planned) vs what they actually spent (actual), by month.
implication: The core accountability mechanism in FP&A. Explains *where* burn is coming from, not just *how much*.

definition: A projected cash path over 9 months. Acme models three: current trajectory, spending discipline, and revenue growth.
implication: Scenarios answer 'what if we do nothing vs cut spend vs grow revenue?' — essential for board planning and fundraising timing.

definition: A flag on a vendor invoice pausing payment pending review — often for duplicates, missing POs, or disputes.
implication: Holds protect cash but create vendor friction. AP team must resolve holds quickly or risk supply chain disruption.

definition: AI review of AP invoices for duplicate charges, suspicious patterns, and unauthorized payments. Unique to the Payables agent.
implication: Catches errors humans miss under volume — e.g. Apex Logistics duplicate invoices ($14.5K). Prevents paying the same bill twice.

definition: How a vendor relationship is structured: annual contract, month-to-month (M2M), or no contract on file.
implication: M2M and no-contract vendors have pricing and renewal risk. High-spend vendors without contracts are negotiation priorities.

definition: Exposure from vendor relationships: spend level, contract coverage, renewal timing, and concentration risk.
implication: A CFO prioritizes renegotiation where spend is high but protection is low — e.g. month-to-month SaaS at $20K+/mo.

definition: Critical finding requiring immediate action. Highest severity in agent output. Auto-creates an action item on Run All.
implication: RED items are your Monday morning priority list. Each should have a dollar impact and a clear decision.

definition: Warning finding — monitor closely, act within days/weeks. Not yet critical but trending wrong.
implication: YELLOW items become RED if ignored. Good for weekly standups and trend tracking.

definition: Healthy finding — no action needed. Confirms something is on track.
implication: GREEN conclusions build trust in the system. Not everything is a fire — the platform also confirms what's working.

definition: A tracked task auto-created from RED agent conclusions. Has owner, due date, amount, and status (open/in-progress/resolved).
implication: Bridges AI insight to human decision. CFO approves, delegates, or dismisses — with consequence preview before committing.

definition: An AI specialist that reads specific financial data, reasons about it, and outputs structured conclusions. Acme has 7 agents.
implication: Think of agents as overnight analysts — each owns a domain (cash, budget, AR, etc.) and reports findings in a standard format.

definition: Executes all 6 specialist agents in parallel, then the CFO Briefing agent sequentially, then creates action items from RED conclusions.
implication: The primary refresh button. Run after data updates or at the start of each week for a fresh executive picture.

definition: Audit section listing exactly which database tables and rows the agent read. Collapsed by default in the UI.
implication: Transparency — you can verify the agent looked at real data, not hallucinated numbers. Critical for trust in finance AI.

definition: Step-by-step logic the agent used to reach conclusions. Shows the 'show your work' behind each finding.
implication: Lets you challenge or validate AI logic. If reasoning is wrong, the conclusion is wrong — even if the number looks right.

definition: AI-generated preview of what happens if you approve an action item — cash impact, runway change, downstream effects.
implication: Decision support before you commit. Turns 'approve this' into 'approve this and here's what it costs/saves'.

definition: The LLM API hosting each agent: Cerebras (fastest), Groq (versatile), or SambaNova (deep reasoning). Shown as colored pills in the UI.
implication: Different models for different jobs — speed for live metrics, depth for fraud detection and executive synthesis.

definition: Financial Planning & Analysis — the team/function that builds budgets, forecasts, and variance reports.
implication: At a firm like Vanguard, FP&A is a core career path. This platform automates the *monitoring* part of FP&A, not the judgment.

definition: A venture funding round. Acme raised $18M in March 2025 (Bessemer). Post-money valuation implies investor expectations for growth.
implication: Series B companies are expected to have product-market fit and focus on scaling — but burn discipline still matters for runway.

definition: At Acme, employees are compensated partly on company performance targets (SLO-based). 47 employees.
implication: Headcount is the largest burn driver. SLO-based comp ties individual incentives to company outcomes.

definition: Flag when an agent conclusion requires C-level attention — typically high-dollar RED items or cross-functional issues.
implication: Not every RED goes to the CEO. Escalation filters what actually needs executive time vs what a controller can handle.
